item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow… the topiary April Fools edit still sounds frightening.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait… if audio recordings work, why not wear a hearing aid that records and reproduces said recording with a small delay?
You would hear them -almost- in real time instead of the 'look away every five minutes' thing… or I got that wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I went ahead and corrected the "visa versa" for a "vice versa". These cases always dumbfound me because I cannot believe that at least a hundred people read the skip before me and nobody noticed that.

Other than that, superb SCP man! It could easily be an episode of Goosebumps. Upvoted

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I'm totally fine with it! I'm kicking myself that I hadn't learned about 2218 before, heh.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a few links to propose here. First off, the mention of everyone's favorite Hazmat based MTF can be turned into a link to their spot on the MTF hub. Additionally, the pathogen featured here is very similar to that seen in SCP-2136 as well as the effects of SCP-2218 instances. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

MTF Beta-7 (Maz Hatters) are to check for instances of SCP-2376-1 in hospitals located in infected areas.

To this:

MTF Beta-7 (Maz Hatters) are to check for instances of SCP-2376-1 in hospitals located in infected areas.

Changing this:

Testing is ongoing around infected areas to determine if SCP-2376 has spread to any other carrier species.

To this:

Testing is ongoing around infected areas to determine if SCP-2376 has spread to any other carrier species. Similarities between the effects of SCP-2376, the SCP-2136 pathogen, and instances of SCP-2218 have been noted. Investigation into whether the anomalous properties affecting SCP-2218 instances are an altered version of SCP-2376, or visa versa, is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part is that the dad apparently must have gone, "Man, I wonder where my son disappeared off t- oh hey, free scarecrow! Neat!"

+1
A creepy as heck skip definitely deserves to be +100 and more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The scarecrow perception happens only AFTER you've been almost completely immobilized by the tetanus strain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the perceived scarecrow is actually 100% fully functional and moving but not perceived as such? It makes more sense to think of them as actually seizing up fully vs them fighting and struggling possibly getting away but still being thrown in a garbage disposal or nailed up as a scare crow.

Initially I was freaked out by
+ show block
– hide block

the father crucifying their son

but than I thought
+ show block
– hide block

'was the son really not going to move his hand away at all or even just curl it away from a nail'

and it got less scary.

I think the deal breaker here is when it was noted they can be cured but still appear as a scarecrow and no movement occurs until not observed. In these instances where a fully functional persons after image is somehow grabbed and manipulated at will is dousing this for me. Especially when you can observe the movements via motion sensors. It think it would be better if it said the traditional treatments show no signs of effect and the disease progresses limiting movement as if it was untreated. That or a experiment log could help but too far down the rabbit hole could make this way worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
STONEFISH YOU'RE BACK!!!!!!!!!!!

And thank you very much! I really appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absentminded, this is the best work of yours I've yet seen. I love it, very classic SCP horror feel.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about 'Scravecrow?'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word scarecrow just lost all meaning.

item.title: Re: Oh god.
item.content:encoded: That's too brilliant. Cold, but brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit. Man, Niles, why couldn't you have been here for the Dystopia Contest? A story about a town of these poor bastards would've been great.

"I know they're people, but… Well, they look like scarecrows. We all do. First thing in the morning, I sit down for my scarecrow breakfast with my scarecrow family, then I get on the bus full of little scarecrows like me, and we go to school and write notes given by our scarecrow teacher. We go out to recess and play scarecrow games (but we don't wander too far because it's easy for us to become Lost Little Scarecrows), then we go home on our bus with our scarecrow driver, eat our dinners, and do our scarecrow homework. We say our scarecrow prayers (and hope that God can hear our scarecrow voices) and go to scarecrow bed.

"I don't know what I'd give to hear my mother's laugh or my father's singing again. I want to hear my baby brother and his silly jokes. I want to be alive again. But I can't. Because we're all scarecrows."

item.title: Re: Oh god.
item.content:encoded:
I always like to imagine scenarios where nonsensical comments like this might make sense.

In this case, I'm seeing a man wandering in a desert. A demon has appeared before him and offered him a pitcher of ice water in exchange for his soul, and asked how long he thinks he can resist the offer.

item.title: Re: Oh god.
item.content:encoded: What are you talking about, Jordan Woods? Your second sentence does not refer to anything, and it makes one question what you mean by the first.

item.title: Re: Oh god.
item.content:encoded:
First of all, this article is actually not that long compared to some other articles out there. It's on the shorter side, especially for Series III articles.

Second, that feedback? Not at all helpful. I have no idea why you downvoted. It's just snarky and doesn't tell me what I need to change. If you're going to give me feedback, at least make it coherent, please. Otherwise, why bother?

item.title: Oh god.
item.content:encoded: Very long. If I was on the edge of death possibly I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When it got to the new strain, thats where things got screwed up.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Shaggy. I really appreciate the praise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing. You had me hooked in the SCProcedures, and held that intrigue throughout. +1. Doubt I'll get sleep tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes - I knew when writing this the procedures would be the draw. I wanted my hook, in particular, to be the last line - the one about reminding staff that they're treating human beings. I think people would be hard pressed to stop reading after that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In particular, the Special Containment Procedures are well-crafted, as well. I've always felt that the Special Containment Procedures are a great tool to force the reader to interact with the object before knowing what it is. As a result, curiosity about what could possibly require these precautions is heightened. These do an excellent job at just that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not meant to kill you. If it just killed you, it wouldn't be scary. The reason why it's so scary is —that it's the people around you who are killing you through inaction.

But I'm glad you liked the article!—

Misread your comment, sorry. Yes, that's exactly what it does - the people around kill you through their lack of action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am impressed. This starts out creepy and moves smoothly into horrific. The worst part, for me, is that it doesn't kill you.

The crowd take care of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terrific fridge horror with the mental image of the father crucifying his son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it in chat and I like it here. Great work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited and fixed - thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor grammatical issue in the description of symptoms - "and as well as". The "and" is redundant. Also, there's a spacing issue around a comma or two later in that same sentence.

Other than that, creepy as frak. The note about the pigeons is what really sold me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed now. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've still got a SCP-XXXX in the containment procedures; other than that, it's pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Magnus. Means a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, they wouldn't. The observers are incapable of perceiving anything that isn't typical behavior of an inanimate scarecrow. They can't hear them talk, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are the observers able to perceive actions done by the instances while keeping their eyes on the image as well?
For example, if an instance walked behind the observer and tapped them on the shoulder or shoved them or something, would they feel it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the instance moves and the observer perceives them as staying still, does that mean they're just looking at an "image" of where the instance was initially?

That's exactly right. If the scarecrow left the room, they would see the scarecrow as remaining wherever it was until they looked away and back again. Then the scarecrow would be gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Motion is not visible to observers, but if a person observing an SCP-2376-1 instance breaks visual contact while the instance is moving, the instance will appear to have moved when the observer regains visual contact.

This sort of confused me. If the instance moves and the observer perceives them as staying still, does that mean they're just looking at an "image" of where the instance was initially?
What happens if the instance moves out of sight from the observer and the observer doesn't break eye contact with their initial position? Does that make the instance practically invisible to the observer, other than the image of them standing still in their original position?
I'm probably overthinking it. Otherwise, it's a great article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creeped me out when you were writing about it, creeps me out now.

Terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here it is! Thanks to SpectralDragon for adding the rating model, and thanks to Sirens, SpectralDragon, DrMagnus, vezaz, Vincent_Redgrave, Lazar, Sciurus, Sleeplight, minmin, Tuomey, Lex1nat0r, and anyone else I'm forgetting for looking at this in chat.

+ Spoilers under the cut
- You found it!

The pathogen and the common crow have a symbiotic relationship. Pathogen gets to spread, crows have an immobile food source that humans won't try to drive them away from. Crows are smart. And what's the biggest city in Illinois?

Enjoy crow's rehedge, motherfuckers.
